title: OTA uploads intermittently fail across cluster
description: Repeated ./ota-all.sh runs are unreliable on the three-board cluster. First run compiled firmware at 118348 bytes / 122880 OTA budget headroom but all three uploads failed: 192.168.8.138, 192.168.8.213, 192.168.8.198. Immediate second run with the same binary succeeded only on 192.168.8.198 and failed on 192.168.8.138 + 192.168.8.213. Logs referenced by ota-all.sh: /tmp/ota-192.168.8.138.log, /tmp/ota-192.168.8.213.log, /tmp/ota-192.168.8.198.log. Investigate whether failures are network reachability, ArduinoOTA authentication/handshake, board web/socket wedge, parallel upload pressure, or firmware OTA receive path. Related context: board 192.168.8.138 has existing chronic reachability issue servo-hem; this broader issue captures OTA flakiness affecting multiple boards.
notes: Diagnostic update 2026-06-01: single-board OTA to 192.168.8.198 with timing instrumentation connected and uploaded the full ~118 KB body, but took the entire 180s window (curl: http_code=000, time_connect=0.005106, time_starttransfer=0, time_total=180.006683, size_upload=118356, speed_upload=657 B/s) and never received an HTTP response. Afterward .198 stopped answering /status.json and ICMP, while .138 and .213 remained reachable. This suggests the flaky behavior is not auth or binary size; it is the custom HTTP OTA receive/stage/apply path being extremely slow/fragile per board. Current local mitigation: sequential uploads, targetable board args, curl timing logs, disabled Expect header, 300s host timeout, and 300s firmware receive deadline. Do not run more OTA against .198 until it is power-cycled or otherwise confirmed recovered.

title: (BUG) Bake produces motions the firmware rejects as bad-track (track must have >=2 keyframes ending at durationMs)
description: Firmware motionParseTrack (motion_engine.h:257) requires every track to have count>=2 keyframes AND the last keyframe.atMs == motionDurationMs, else the whole MOTION fails to load with 'MOTION failed: bad-track'. The browser does NOT enforce this: normalizeKeyframes only synthesizes a start boundary (motions 'end on the last explicit keyframe', servo % work) and allows single-keyframe tracks (the default track is [{atMs:0,value:0}]). sliceForBoard bakes ALL of a board's tracks, so a motion where the operator edited only the DC track still bakes 3 single-keyframe servo tracks → firmware rejects bad-track → baked playback is impossible. This blocked the baked-vs-live DC A/B (servo-a1e.1) and explains servo-67d operators 'couldn't invoke' baked motions.
acceptance_criteria: A motion authored in the editor (including default single-keyframe tracks and a last keyframe before durationMs) bakes to a blob the firmware loads and plays without bad-track. Either the bake conforms tracks to the >=2-keyframes/last-at-duration contract, or pre-bake validation blocks+explains it. Verified on hardware: MOTION <id> plays after bake.
notes: Observed 2026-05-29 over USB: 'MOTION up-down-up-down' on board 1 (.198) returned 'MOTION failed: bad-track'. Motion had an authored DC track plus default single-keyframe servo tracks. Two firmware rules to satisfy: (1) >=2 keyframes per track, (2) last keyframe atMs == motion durationMs. Fix options: browser bake should conform each baked track (synthesize a trailing keyframe at durationMs holding the last value; drop or pad single-keyframe/default tracks), and/or add pre-bake validation that surfaces the offending track BEFORE POST. Firmware-side relaxation (accept single-keyframe hold tracks / last<duration) is the alternative but costs flash and changes the interpolation contract.
close_reason: Bake now conforms each track to the firmware keyframe contract (>=2 kf, first at 0, last at durationMs) via conformTrackForBake in sliceForBoard; trailing pad holds last value; over-budget motions (>64 total keyframes / >17 tracks) blocked+named via analyzeBakeConformance. Editor model untouched, no firmware change. PR opened. Verified: node unit tests, live preview (default+multi-kf valid, 77-kf blocked), 6/6 motion tests, console clean.

title: Motion editor: constrain keyframe edits to physically possible servo timing
description: Apply the feasibility model while authoring. Dragging or typed edits that make an adjacent servo segment faster than the measured mechanical limit should be clamped to the nearest feasible time/value or rejected with an inline error. The rule must consider both neighbors of the edited keyframe, since moving one point can invalidate the incoming or outgoing segment.
acceptance_criteria: Dragging a servo keyframe cannot leave a red/impossible segment without explicit rejection; typed time/value edits show the minimum needed duration and either clamp or refuse the edit. DC tracks are not constrained by servo slew rules. Existing snap behavior still works.
notes: Editor normalization no longer forces a trailing 0% boundary. New motions start at 0 but hold the last explicit keyframe unless the author adds an explicit return segment.
close_reason: Servo-feasible keyframe authoring shipped on main (servo-iga/servo-79q): SERVO_FEASIBILITY_MS_PER_PERCENT=77 model used by render/drag/play/bake, ok/warn/invalid/hold timeline styling + tooltips, Play Live/Bake blocking with segment messages, and drag clamping to feasible timing (lowerBound/upperBound + impossible rejection). Closing per operator decision; remaining acceptance clauses (lzn.1 automated test harness, lzn.5 full hardware guardrail matrix beyond the recorded servo-ao9 slew observation) accepted as-is.

title: Motion editor: block Play Live and Bake when servo segments are impossible
description: Before Play Live, Fire MOTION, export, or bake-to-board actions, validate every servo segment in every Motion being sent. Impossible segments should block the action and identify the track, from/to values, segment duration, and required minimum duration. Slow/staccato segments should warn but remain allowed.
acceptance_criteria: A Motion containing 0%->50% in 2000ms cannot be played live or baked; the UI points to the offending segment and says it needs about 3850ms. A 0%->50% in 6000ms segment passes. A very slow full-range segment warns about possible staccato but can be intentionally kept.
notes: Play Live, MOTION fire, bake, and export now inspect servo feasibility before sending. Invalid segments block the action with a track-specific requirement message; slow/staccato segments log or display warnings but remain allowed. Remaining dependency is servo-lzn.1 because the gating uses that shared analyzer.
close_reason: Servo-feasible keyframe authoring shipped on main (servo-iga/servo-79q): SERVO_FEASIBILITY_MS_PER_PERCENT=77 model used by render/drag/play/bake, ok/warn/invalid/hold timeline styling + tooltips, Play Live/Bake blocking with segment messages, and drag clamping to feasible timing (lowerBound/upperBound + impossible rejection). Closing per operator decision; remaining acceptance clauses (lzn.1 automated test harness, lzn.5 full hardware guardrail matrix beyond the recorded servo-ao9 slew observation) accepted as-is.

title: Motion editor: implement servo segment feasibility model
description: Create shared browser-side helpers for servo track feasibility. For each adjacent servo keyframe segment, compute deltaPercent=abs(to.value-from.value) and minDurationMs=ceil(deltaPercent*77). Also compute a slow/staccato warning threshold, initially ceil(deltaPercent*120), and treat tiny deltas below roughly 2% as holds rather than meaningful slow motion. The model must use absolute percent-down values, not relative commands.
acceptance_criteria: servo_controller.html has a single helper used by render, drag/type validation, live playback, and bake validation. Unit-ish browser tests or a small deterministic test harness cover 0->25 needing about 1925ms, 0->50 needing about 3850ms, 25->50 needing about 1925ms, 0->100 needing about 7700ms, and long full-range moves warning above about 12000ms.
notes: Implemented shared feasibility helpers in servo_controller.html: segment analysis uses deltaPercent*77ms minimum duration, >deltaPercent*120ms slow/staccato warnings, and <2% deltas as holds. The helper now drives render-time lane overlays, Play Live gating, MOTION fire gating, bake/export validation, and drag/click authoring constraints. Remaining gap: no standalone browser test harness yet.
close_reason: Servo-feasible keyframe authoring shipped on main (servo-iga/servo-79q): SERVO_FEASIBILITY_MS_PER_PERCENT=77 model used by render/drag/play/bake, ok/warn/invalid/hold timeline styling + tooltips, Play Live/Bake blocking with segment messages, and drag clamping to feasible timing (lowerBound/upperBound + impossible rejection). Closing per operator decision; remaining acceptance clauses (lzn.1 automated test harness, lzn.5 full hardware guardrail matrix beyond the recorded servo-ao9 slew observation) accepted as-is.

title: Motion editor: servo-feasible keyframe authoring
description: Add guardrails so the browser Motion editor only allows servo keyframe timelines that the installed goBILDA 5-turn winches can physically follow. The model should use the measured slew-rate floor from servo-ao9: about 7700ms for 100% travel, or 77ms per percent of absolute travel. Validation must run per segment, because a Motion can be globally long but still impossible if one adjacent keyframe jump is too fast.
acceptance_criteria: The Motion editor has a documented feasibility model for servo segments; child tasks cover validation, editing constraints, play/bake gating, and hardware verification. Impossible servo segments cannot be silently baked or played as if valid.
close_reason: Servo-feasible keyframe authoring shipped on main (servo-iga/servo-79q): SERVO_FEASIBILITY_MS_PER_PERCENT=77 model used by render/drag/play/bake, ok/warn/invalid/hold timeline styling + tooltips, Play Live/Bake blocking with segment messages, and drag clamping to feasible timing (lowerBound/upperBound + impossible rejection). Closing per operator decision; remaining acceptance clauses (lzn.1 automated test harness, lzn.5 full hardware guardrail matrix beyond the recorded servo-ao9 slew observation) accepted as-is.

title: Hardware smoke: verify browser Gallery toggle across cluster
description: Follow-up from servo-9li. After flashing firmware with /status.json gallery support, open servo_controller.html, confirm the masthead Gallery toggle reflects all reachable boards, confirm ON asks for confirmation, verify all reachable boards report gallery=true, reboot to confirm the flag persists and auto-run behavior is unchanged, then toggle OFF and verify gallery=false on all reachable boards.
close_reason: Hardware cluster smoke completed by operator: browser Gallery toggle verified across reachable boards after firmware with /status.json gallery support; ON confirmation, persistent gallery=true/false reporting, reboot persistence, and OFF path accepted.

title: Board 138 chronically wedges / needs power-cycle — investigate UNO R4 WiFi reachability loss
description:
Board at 192.168.8.138 (board 3, historically the slowest — see servo-3k3 memory) repeatedly becomes unreachable until a manual power-cycle. Confirmed it serves nothing: curl -m12 to /status.json returns HTTP 000 (no bytes) while the other two boards (198/213) are fine. Power-cycling restores it. Recurring — blocks bakes and testing.

DIFFERENTIAL (UNO R4 WiFi):
1. Power/brownout from servo current (shared supply sags rail → MCU/ESP32-S3 radio dies). Tell: correlates with movement; uptimeMs resets.
2. Single-socket server starvation / connection leak: controller polls /status.json + /boardId for all boards every ~800ms; the 4s status fetch (STATUS_TIMEOUT_MS=4000) aborts on the slowest board (138) and can leave half-open sockets → server stops accepting. Tell: wedges only while controller polling / after bake; survives idle.
3. Wi-Fi drop without clean recovery (weakest-signal board). Tell: wedges independent of load; Serial shows disconnect.
4. Heap fragmentation over hours (status.json builds a String each poll; ~10KB free RAM). Tell: only after long uptime.

EXPERIMENTS TO LOCALIZE (do before any code change):
- Idle test: close the controller (no polling), leave 138 powered untouched. Still wedges → power/wifi/heap; stays up → controller polling load implicated.
- On next wedge, before power-cycle: open Serial over USB. Sketch alive but no HTTP → network-stack wedge; Serial dead / uptimeMs ~0 on return → crash/brownout.
- Confirm power topology: is 138's logic board on a supply separate from servo power?

POSSIBLE SOFTWARE MITIGATIONS (only if experiment #1 implicates polling load):
- Reduce poll pressure: don't fetch /boardId every poll (refreshBoardIdMap), back off polling a board after repeated failures, or lengthen the poll interval.
- Reconsider STATUS_TIMEOUT_MS=4000 (still the laggard vs /boardId's 8s after servo-fee) — abort-on-slow-board may be leaking the board's sockets.
- Firmware: ensure every webPoll path client.stop()s even on partial/aborted requests; consider a socket/heap watchdog that restarts WiFi/server when the stack wedges.
design: No code change yet — this is Phase-1 evidence gathering. Run the idle test + Serial-on-wedge check to localize to power vs polling-load vs wifi before proposing a fix. If power: separate clean supply for the logic board. If polling-load: reduce controller poll pressure + verify firmware closes sockets on every path. If wifi: harden the reconnect path (servo-974 added some recovery; may be incomplete). A firmware watchdog that detects a wedged WiFi/server and restarts network services would be a defense-in-depth backstop.
acceptance_criteria: Root cause localized via the idle + Serial experiments and documented. The chosen mitigation keeps 138 reachable through a normal test/bake session without a manual power-cycle (verify across a multi-hour run and a full bake cycle).

title: Sequencer UI: make step authoring motion-first; demote raw low-level commands
description:
Design question raised during servo-dos testing: the // 04 Sequencer step-command palette exposes low-level commands (ROTATE, MOVE, DOWN/UP, S<n>, RIG) as equals alongside MOTION <id>. For finished installation choreography these are largely redundant with Motions — anything they do to servos/DC a Motion track expresses better (keyframe editor, feasibility checks, scrub/preview). Exposing the full palette invites bypassing the motion editor and creates two-ways-to-do-the-same-thing.

INTENDED MENTAL MODEL (keep): Motion = expressive primitive; Sequence = timed arrangement of motions; Setlist = list of sequences looping = long strings of actions.

DO NOT remove firmware capability: the sequence runner is a generic command dispatcher and a few step commands are NOT motions and must stay supported — STOP (gap/silence inside a sequence), a bare ROTATE hold, board-targeted one-offs (step.target=1/2/3), quick poses while prototyping. This is a UI-surface change, not a schema/firmware change.

PROPOSED UI (servo_controller.html // 04): step-cmd dropdown leads with the library's MOTION <id> entries + STOP (motion-first default); tuck raw movement commands (ROTATE/MOVE/RIG/S0..) behind an 'advanced' optgroup or toggle so they're available but de-emphasized. Keep STOP first-class. Consider whether RUN <id> (sequence nesting) should be offered at all (currently excluded from suggestions; nesting a whole sequence in a step is confusing).
design:
REFINED APPROACH (operator request): replace the closed <select data-field=cmd> with an editable combobox — <input data-field=cmd list=seqCmdSuggest> + a shared <datalist id=seqCmdSuggest>. The datalist suggests ONLY the library's MOTION <id>s + STOP (clean, motion-first); the operator can type any other command (e.g. ROTATE 24, DOWN 0 50, board-targeted one-offs) freely. readSequenceFromDom already reads .value, so it works unchanged for an <input>.

ALSO FIXES A LATENT DATA-LOSS BUG: the current closed <select> + commandOptions() only renders preset/motion values as <option>s. A step whose cmd is any non-preset command (custom ROTATE/DOWN values, imported/pulled sequences) has NO matching option, so the select falls back to its first option and the next save (readSequenceFromDom → select.value) SILENTLY REWRITES the step's command. The combobox eliminates this because the input holds the literal value regardless of suggestions. This is why presets like 'ROTATE 30' existed but 'ROTATE 24' was unreachable.

Replaces the earlier 'advanced optgroup' idea. No firmware/schema change — firmware still accepts any command string (schema §3). Touch points: servo_controller.html step row markup (~line 4213), commandOptions() → becomes a datalist-options builder (motions + STOP), and add the <datalist> element. Verify in preview: custom 'ROTATE 24' typed into a step survives a save/reload round-trip; datalist shows motions + STOP; no console errors.
acceptance_criteria: Step cmd field is an editable combobox: datalist suggests the library's MOTION <id>s + STOP; operator can type any command and it is preserved verbatim through save/reload (no silent rewrite of non-preset commands); firmware still accepts any baked command string; no console errors.
notes: Implemented in PR #41: https://github.com/tatecarson/adafruit_16_servo/pull/41 (base main). Editable combobox (input+datalist) suggesting baked motions only; free-type any command; hover help (CMD_HELP) on cmd header ⓘ + each input. Fixes the closed-select data-loss bug (ROTATE 24 now survives save/reload). No firmware/schema change. Verified in preview.
close_reason: Merged to main (PR #41). Editable command combobox (datalist suggests baked motions; free-type any command) + hover command help; fixed the closed-<select> data-loss bug (custom commands like 'ROTATE 24' now survive save/reload). Verified in preview. Done.

title: Bake: pause status polling during bake + resilient boardId resolve (stop false 'unreachable')
description: The controller polls /status.json on every board every ~800ms via setInterval(pollOnce) and never pauses it, including during a bake. The UNO R4 WiFi is single-threaded and a bake blocks its web server for 60-120s while writing dataflash; hammering a busy board with status polls congests its single connection socket, so concurrent /boardId resolves and POSTs time out and boards are falsely reported 'unreachable'. Plus fetchBoardId uses a hard 4s timeout with no retry, declaring a momentarily-busy-but-alive board dead. Fix: (1) stop polling for the duration of bake/retry and resume after (finally); (2) bump the boardId resolve timeout and retry once before giving up.
acceptance_criteria: Status polling is paused during bake/retry and resumed afterward (even on early return/failure). boardId resolution retries with a longer timeout before declaring a board unreachable. Fewer false 'unreachable' reports during bakes. JS syntax passes.
notes: Surfaced 2026-05-29 — board .213 repeatedly reported unreachable mid-bake while online in the router. Root cause is self-inflicted polling congestion on top of the R4's slow blocking EEPROM + flaky WiFi. Firmware-side WiFi flakiness is separate.
close_reason: Bake now pauses status polling for its duration (stopPolling at start, startPolling in finally on every exit) so we stop congesting single-threaded boards mid-write; boardId resolve timeout raised 4s→8s with one retry. Preview-verified: polling paused during bake and resumed after, failed boards tracked, console clean. Ships in PR #32.

title: Bake: Retry Failed button to re-bake only the boards that failed
description: EEPROM writes are slow (60-120s/board), so when one board fails a bake (timeout/unreachable) re-baking all three to fix one wastes minutes. Add a Retry Failed button next to Bake to Boards that re-bakes only the boards that failed (POST error or unreachable) in the last run. bakeToAllBoards takes an optional onlyIps subset; failures are tracked in lastBakeFailedIps; the button enables when there are failures and disables otherwise.
acceptance_criteria: A Retry Failed button re-bakes only the boards that failed/were unreachable in the last bake; disabled when none failed; updates per-board snapshots on success. JS syntax passes; verified in preview.
close_reason: Retry Failed button added: bakeToAllBoards(onlyIps) bakes a subset; lastBakeFailedIps tracks POST/unreachable failures; button enables only when failures exist. Preview-verified (present, disabled by default, empty-guard, console clean); retry reuses the proven POST loop. Ships in PR #32.

title: Bake reports false failure when client times out mid-write (retry /sequences/info with backoff)
description: POST /sequences can exceed BAKE_TIMEOUT_MS (150s) on the slowest boards because UNO R4 dataflash writes are slow; the write still lands. postSequences then runs ONE recovery probe (fetchSequencesInfo, INFO_TIMEOUT_MS=30s) and declares 'failed — bake not confirmed' if that single probe doesn't confirm bytesUsed — but the board is often still writing and unresponsive at that moment, so the probe times out even though the bake succeeds. Operator impact: bake log says 'failed', so the operator re-bakes (another 2+ min) or assumes breakage. Fix: after a client timeout, retry GET /sequences/info a few times with backoff (the board becomes responsive once the write completes), and only declare failure if bytesUsed still doesn't match after the retries.
acceptance_criteria: A bake whose write lands but whose initial recovery probe times out is reported as ok/recovered, not failed. postSequences retries /sequences/info with backoff before declaring failure. No false 'failed' on a confirmed bytesUsed match.
notes: Observed 2026-05-29: board 192.168.8.198 POST 3367B reported 'client timeout, bake not confirmed', but GET /sequences/info immediately after showed bytesUsed=3367 + hasActive=true — the write had landed. See servo_controller.html postSequences() recovery path and BAKE_TIMEOUT_MS / INFO_TIMEOUT_MS. Related memory: servo-3k3.
close_reason: Implemented bake timeout recovery retries for /sequences/info with backoff and documented host test coverage.

title: Motion editor: New should prompt for a name (derive id at creation)
description: Clicking New immediately creates 'New Motion' / 'new-motion', forcing a separate Rename ID step to get a descriptive id. New should prompt for the motion name up front and derive the id from it, so most motions never need Rename ID. Cancel should abort creation; empty falls back to the default.
acceptance_criteria: Clicking New prompts for a name; the created motion uses that name and an id slugged from it (deduped). Cancel creates nothing. JS syntax passes; browser smoke test confirms the flow.
close_reason: New now prompts for a name up front and derives the id from it via defaultMotion(lib,name); cancel aborts creation, blank falls back to 'New Motion'. Verified in browser: named→slug id, cancel→no-op, blank→default; console clean.

title: Motion editor: editable, descriptive Motion IDs
description: Motion ids are auto-generated ('new-motion', 'new-motion-2', ...) and immutable in the UI. The Name field only edits the display name; the id (the key used by localStorage, the dropdown, and the 'MOTION <id>' play command) can't be changed. Operators can't organize motions with descriptive ids. Add: (1) a read-only current-id display in the editor, (2) a Rename ID action that slugifies+dedupes a new id, auto-rewrites referencing Sequence steps ('MOTION <old>'->'MOTION <new>'), and surfaces a re-bake-needed note, (3) derive the id from the name at creation.
design: Decisions (brainstorm 2026-05-29): id slugged from name at creation + STABLE through name edits; explicit Rename ID control (repurpose existing #motionRename button). Rename auto-rewrites sequence step cmd references and reports count. Show 're-bake needed' status note after rename since boards still hold the old baked id. Name field continues to edit display name only.
acceptance_criteria: Editor shows the current motion id. A Rename ID action changes the id via slugify+dedupe, rewrites every Sequence step whose cmd is 'MOTION <old-id>' to the new id (with a count in status), and shows a re-bake reminder. New motions get an id derived from their name. JS syntax check passes.
close_reason: Implemented in servo_controller.html: read-only 'id: <slug>' chip in the motion picker, Rename ID action (repurposed #motionRename) that slugifies+dedupes via uniqueMotionId, auto-rewrites Sequence step 'MOTION <old>' refs via rewriteMotionIdReferences (exact id-token match, preserves LOOP args / sibling ids), shows count + re-bake note; defaultMotion now derives id from name. Verified: JS syntax OK, node unit tests for slug/dedupe/ref-rewrite pass, browser smoke test (id chip renders 110px, rename rewrites 1 seq ref, status correct, console clean).

title: Baked motions should surface clearly as playable browser/sequence options
description: Operator baked a Motion from the editor but could not reliably confirm or invoke it afterward. The baked motion did not clearly surface as an available option in the sequencer dropdown, which blocked comparison testing between Play Live and baked MOTION playback. The current UI makes motion id vs name too opaque and does not provide a trustworthy post-bake confirmation path for which baked motion ids are available on which boards.
acceptance_criteria: A beads issue exists for baked-motion discoverability. Scope should cover: clear labeling of motion id vs display name, immediate browser UI refresh after save/bake, and a reliable way to confirm which baked MOTION ids are available to run.
close_reason: Added 'Baked on boards' panel (// 03) that reads the per-ip bake snapshot (LIB_SNAP_PREFIX) and lists each baked motion with name+id labeling and a Play button firing MOTION <id> to that board; per-board 'edited since bake' staleness flag (diffBlob vs current slice); refreshes after save/bake. Browser-snapshot source of truth, no firmware change. Cross-browser file persistence split to servo-1xt. PR #30. Verified in preview: renders per-board, Play targets correct ip, staleness toggles, console clean.

title: Investigate board availability mismatch with admin panel
description: Boards often appear online in the router's admin page, but servo_controller.html does not reliably treat them as available until the Arduinos are power-cycled. Symptom: the router shows the devices on the network, while the browser UI shows them offline or unusable for control. Investigate whether this is stale browser-side board discovery, cached IP/board state, HTTP endpoint readiness after boot, mDNS/name-resolution issues, or a firmware/network recovery problem after reconnects.
acceptance_criteria: A beads task exists for the router-admin-vs-browser availability mismatch. Investigation should compare what the router considers 'online' with what servo_controller.html requires before marking a board usable, identify the failure mode that makes power-cycling temporarily fix it, and propose either a browser-side recovery path or a firmware/network fix.
close_reason: Identified likely mismatch: WiFi reconnect restored router presence but firmware did not restart HTTP/OTA/UDP listeners. Firmware now restarts network services after reconnect and keeps WiFi recovery running after USB-only boot; hardware reconnect smoke remains documented.

title: Manual test: measure DC live-playback throughput limits
description: Before implementing DC keyframe guardrails, measure the practical throughput limit for DC live playback. Dense DC keyframes appear to outrun the browser-to-board HTTP path during Play Live, leaving motor state lagging behind the playhead by the end of the motion. Run a manual test matrix that varies event density, duration, and value delta size, then compare Play Live against baked MOTION playback to determine whether the limit is transport-side, firmware-side, or both.
acceptance_criteria: docs/TESTING.md records a repeatable DC test matrix with at least 4/8/12/16 keyframes over 8s and 4s, covering small, medium, and large ROTATE deltas. Results identify the approximate minimum safe spacing between DC events for Play Live, note whether baked MOTION behaves differently, and provide enough data to drive Motion-editor warnings or invalid states.
notes: Manual HTML-page findings from operator testing: over 8s, 4 DC keyframes performs acceptably except large/fast jumps toward 100 should likely be guarded; 8 keyframes is very laggy and seems to realize only about 2 of the speed changes; 12 and 16 keyframes not completed. Over 4s, 4 keyframes realized only about 2 changes; 8 keyframes realized about 4 changes; 12 and 16 not completed. Delta families tested/planned: small 0->15->0, medium 0->50->-50, large 100->-100. Baked MOTION comparison was blocked because the baked motion was not surfacing as a usable option / could not be confidently invoked.
close_reason: docs/TESTING.md Test 31 records the DC live-playback throughput matrix (4/8/12/16 kf over 8s and 4s, small/medium/large deltas) from operator testing; derived ~2000ms safe spacing / 1200ms block floor. Baked-vs-live A/B noted as blocked on servo-67d.

title: Motion editor: model DC live-playback throughput limits
description: Dense DC motor keyframes can outrun the browser-to-board HTTP command budget during Play Live. Unlike servo tracks, which now dispatch one DMOVE per segment, DC tracks still emit a stream of ROTATE setpoint changes. When too many DC keyframes occur within a short duration, the board processes them late and the motor state lags behind the playhead by the end of the motion. Add a DC-specific feasibility/throughput model for live playback: measure the practical minimum spacing between ROTATE events, warn or block DC keyframe densities that exceed it, and distinguish browser live-playback limits from baked MOTION playback limits. Consider whether adjacent DC keyframes should be compressed/coalesced into a scheduled event queue rather than naive live streaming.
acceptance_criteria: A beads task exists that explicitly covers DC live-playback throughput limits. The task calls for measuring/defining a minimum safe ROTATE event spacing, surfacing warnings or invalid states in the Motion editor for over-dense DC keyframes, and clarifying whether baked playback has different limits from Play Live.
close_reason: Implemented DC live-playback throughput model in servo_controller.html: DC_LIVE_WARN_SPACING_MS=2000 / DC_LIVE_MIN_SPACING_MS=1200, analyzeMotionDcLiveFeasibility(), DC timeline markers, and Play Live block on over-dense DC. Scoped to live HTTP path only — baked MOTION/bake path stays servo-slew-only. Matrix + thresholds documented in docs/TESTING.md Test 31.

title: Manual test: verify servo feasibility guardrails on hardware
description: After the feasibility guardrails land, test against the live winch hardware. Confirm the editor blocks impossible segments before they move the sculpture and still allows visually distinct feasible segments.
acceptance_criteria: Manual test notes in docs/TESTING.md cover at least: 0->25 in 1000ms blocked; 0->25 in 3000ms allowed and visibly slower than max; 0->50 in 2000ms blocked; 0->50 in 6000ms allowed; 0->100 in 15000ms allowed with staccato warning. Include board IP/id and observed behavior.
close_reason: Servo-feasible keyframe authoring shipped on main (servo-iga/servo-79q): SERVO_FEASIBILITY_MS_PER_PERCENT=77 model used by render/drag/play/bake, ok/warn/invalid/hold timeline styling + tooltips, Play Live/Bake blocking with segment messages, and drag clamping to feasible timing (lowerBound/upperBound + impossible rejection). Closing per operator decision; remaining acceptance clauses (lzn.1 automated test harness, lzn.5 full hardware guardrail matrix beyond the recorded servo-ao9 slew observation) accepted as-is.

title: Motion editor: visualize servo segment feasibility on automation lanes
description: Render feasibility directly on the Motion editor tracks so operators understand why a keyframe is allowed or blocked. Segment lines should indicate ok, too-fast/impossible, slow/staccato warning, and hold/tiny-delta states without relying only on text. Hover/focus details should show delta percent, duration, and minimum required duration.
acceptance_criteria: Servo track segments render with distinct ok/warning/invalid/hold styling; invalid styling appears immediately when a segment violates delta*77ms; tooltip or inspector copy explains the exact requirement. The visualization updates during drag before release.
notes: Implemented motion-segment overlays on servo tracks with ok/warning/invalid/hold styling and tooltip text from the shared feasibility analyzer. Overlays recompute during drag because renderMotionEditor now rebuilds them from the current draft motion state.
close_reason: Servo-feasible keyframe authoring shipped on main (servo-iga/servo-79q): SERVO_FEASIBILITY_MS_PER_PERCENT=77 model used by render/drag/play/bake, ok/warn/invalid/hold timeline styling + tooltips, Play Live/Bake blocking with segment messages, and drag clamping to feasible timing (lowerBound/upperBound + impossible rejection). Closing per operator decision; remaining acceptance clauses (lzn.1 automated test harness, lzn.5 full hardware guardrail matrix beyond the recorded servo-ao9 slew observation) accepted as-is.

title: Document servo slew-rate floor (~7.7s full sweep) for motion authoring
description: The goBILDA 5-Turn winches on channels 0-2 have a measured mechanical slew-rate floor of ~7.7-7.8 seconds for full pulse-range sweep. DMOVE durations BELOW this floor are clamped to the floor (firmware finishes ramping but servo physically lags). DMOVE durations ABOVE the floor are honored but produce staccato motion because each commanded pulse delta is below the servo's dead-band, so the servo accumulates a few μs of error then fires a max-speed correction. Operators authoring multi-keyframe motions need to know this to set realistic segment durations.
acceptance_criteria: docs/sequencer-schema.md (or a new docs/motion-authoring.md) documents: (1) measured floor ~7.7s for goBILDA 5-Turn winches at min=98 max=485 calibration; (2) above-floor durations produce staccato motion due to servo dead-band; (3) practical recommendation: 4-7s segments produce smooth max-speed motion; >12s segments produce visible staccato that may or may not be desirable for the gallery aesthetic. PR #25 commit message or README updated to point at the doc.
notes:
Empirical data from PR #25 testing on hardware (board 198, S0 calibrated 480-2370us): 
DMOVE 0 0 1000      = 7.71s physical
DMOVE 0 100 1000    = 7.78s physical
DMOVE 0 0 6000      = 7.80s physical (still floor-limited)
DMOVE 0 100 6000    = 7.66s physical (still floor-limited)
DMOVE 0 0 15000     = ~15s physical, visible staccato
DMOVE 0 100 15000   = ~15s physical, visible staccato
Firmware verified correct via STATUS captures showing pos progressing linearly through the requested duration; the physical lag is purely the servo's mechanical max slew. No firmware change needed; this is a documentation / authoring-guidance task. See servo_control.h moveServoAnimated for the firmware path.
close_reason: Documented servo slew-rate floor and motion-authoring guidance

title: Motion editor: dispatch DMOVE per-segment instead of DOWN per-tick for smooth servo motion
description: User report: 8-second 0->100% servo sweep in motion editor moves erratically (start/stop pattern) because the browser dispatches DOWN <ch> <pct> at 5Hz. DOWN calls setServoPercent which writes the pulse instantly — servo slams toward each commanded position, then sits idle 100-150ms waiting for the next command. The firmware already has moveServoPercent(servo, pct, duration_ms) that linearly interpolates pulse over a duration, but no command line wires it to DMOVE/UMOVE/MOVE. Solution is two-part: firmware adds DMOVE <ch> <pct> <duration_ms> command; browser changes the motion dispatch to send ONE DMOVE per keyframe segment instead of streaming DOWN at 5Hz. Network traffic drops ~20x, servo motion is smooth (firmware does linear interp natively).
acceptance_criteria: Firmware accepts DMOVE <ch> <pct> <duration_ms> and animates the servo over the requested duration via moveServoPercent. Browser motion editor live-playback sends one DMOVE per segment between keyframes (e.g. 3 keyframes = 2 commands, not 40). 8-second single-segment sweep moves visibly smoothly with no detectable start/stop pattern at the servo. Scrubbing still uses DOWN for single-point updates. Pre-roll glide on Play continues to work (probably remains DOWN-streamed at 5Hz since it's a short interpolation between the servo's current position and motion start).
notes: Discovered while testing PR #23 motion editor on hardware. DMOVE/UMOVE were mentioned in older docs/TESTING.md but apparently never landed in command_interface.h. moveServoPercent() function in servo_runtime.h is the existing target — already used internally by the MOTION engine. Servo-e3t (per-segment curves) will need this dispatch model anyway for linear segments; non-linear curves can fall back to per-tick DOWN. Largely supersedes servo-0bt (HOL blocking) — with per-segment dispatch the HTTP rate is so low that HOL stalls are no longer a concern.
close_reason: Shipped in PR #25 (344fdf8 on main). Firmware DMOVE/UMOVE animated commands + browser per-segment dispatch + linear easing. Hardware testing confirmed firmware works correctly; perceived 'DMOVE ignores duration' was the servo mechanical max-slew rate (~7.7s full sweep). Operator-facing slew-rate docs tracked as servo-ao9.

title: Firmware servo_setup: downDegrees=1500 limits DOWN <ch> 100 to 4.17 turns instead of full 5-turn travel
description: In adafruit_16_servo/servo_setup.h applyCustomServoSetup() sets servoConfig[0..2].downDegrees=1500 with a stale '// TODO: calibrate actual winch travel' comment. percentToDegrees(N, 100) maps to downDegrees, so DOWN 0 100 commands position 1500° which is 4.17 turns of a 5-turn winch — operator authoring motion at 100% only gets 83% of the mechanical travel they expect. totalDegrees=1800 is correct (5 × 360°).
acceptance_criteria: DOWN 0 100 / DOWN 1 100 / DOWN 2 100 reach the full 5-turn extent (1800°). Authoring a motion keyframe at value=100% with the // 06 Motion editor produces the full mechanical sweep on each of the three winches.
notes: Observed during servo-6oa Test A2 follow-up — user reported 'DOWN 0 100 only does 4 rotations instead of 5'. Simple config fix in servo_setup.h. May break any baked sequence that intentionally relied on the reduced range, but those would have been authored with the wrong UX semantic anyway.
close_reason: Shipped in PR #24 (c2bbebd on main). downDegrees=1800 for ch 0/1/2 — DOWN <ch> 0 → DOWN <ch> 100 now sweeps the full 5-turn extent on the goBILDA winches.

title: Firmware: baked MOTION servo tracks should use percent winch semantics
description: The Motion editor now authors winch servo tracks as absolute 0..100% down values, and the feasibility model depends on percent deltas. Baked firmware playback must match the browser live-preview path (DMOVE/DOWN percent semantics) instead of treating keyframe values as 0..180 degrees or clamping them before sequenceDegreesToPulse. Today motionApplyServo still clamps to 0..180 and maps through the older degree path, so baked MOTION playback can disagree with the editor and with feasibility validation.
acceptance_criteria: For servo tracks in baked schema-v1 Motions, value=0 maps to the configured fully-up/retracted endpoint and value=100 maps to the configured fully-down/lowered endpoint using the same percentToDegrees/down-range semantics as DOWN/DMOVE. Intermediate values map linearly across configured travel. Existing saved degree-era motions are explicitly migrated or documented as legacy. Host tests cover 0, 50, and 100 percent on a 5-turn winch config. docs/sequencer-schema.md is updated so servo Motion values are documented as percent, not degrees.
notes: Updated after servo-ao9/servo-lzn: baked playback must align with the physical-feasibility model, which is based on absolute percent keyframe deltas. This supersedes the earlier downDegrees/pass-through framing.
close_reason: Baked MOTION servo tracks now use 0..100% winch semantics via percentToDegrees/degreesToPulse; host motion tests updated and passing; schema and README aligned.

title: Motion editor: percentage UX for servo tracks (0..100%) instead of degrees (0..180)
description: Discovered during servo-2n9 manual testing. The Motion editor's servo track specs are 'min: 0, max: 180, unit: deg' but the firmware treats channels 0/1/2 as 5-turn winches with totalDegrees=1800, so the motion editor only exercises 10% of each servo's calibrated pulse range. Symptom on hardware: S0 180 produces only a tiny movement because 180/1800 = 10% of travel.
design: Servo tracks become {min: 0, max: 100, unit: '%'}. Editor sends 'UP <pct>' to each board for its servo channels (or extend with a target-channel arg if UP is global). DC tracks (-100..100 speed) unchanged. Calibration UI still calibrates minUs/maxUs/offsetDeg pulse range; the percent → pulse mapping happens inside firmware via servoConfig.totalDegrees + up/downDegrees + reverseDir, which already exists.
acceptance_criteria: // 06 Motion editor servo tracks display 0..100% on the value axis with unit '%'. Keyframes store value as percent (0..100). When dispatching a frame, the editor sends either UP <pct>/DOWN <pct> (existing firmware path that uses percentToDegrees against the calibrated up/downDegrees range) OR a new SPCT <ch> <pct> command. Existing motion library entries with deg values need a one-time migration to percent (clamp 0..180 → 0..10% or scale 0..1800 → 0..100). Schema doc updated.
notes: Discovered while testing servo-2n9 (firmware calibration). User confirmed winches are intentional hardware; the motion editor UX needs to follow. Existing UP/DOWN commands already implement percentage->degrees->pulse via percentToDegrees in servo_control.h — likely no firmware change needed, just browser side.
close_reason: Shipped in PR #23 (ce89ba0 on main). Servo tracks switched to 0..100% down semantic, dispatched via DOWN <ch> <pct> through firmware's percentToDegrees + calibrated up/downDegrees; DC tracks step between keyframes, servo tracks ramp; legacy 0..180 degree motions auto-migrate on load; 15% value-axis inset prevents adjacent-row diamond overlap; pre-roll glide from last-known position to motion start (dispatch-completion exit). Follow-ups filed: servo-l8l (firmware MOTION engine clamp 0..180 for baked playback), servo-0bt (HTTP HOL blocking transport).

title: Firmware: persisted servo calibration (Serial CAL_* commands, no HTTP)
description: Firmware-only carve-out of servo-ehd. Add per-channel calibration that persists across reboots: minUs, maxUs, offsetDeg (signed). 16-byte packed record at EEPROM offset 8176 (uses the previously-unused 16-byte tail after slot 1). Apply calibration in S<n> handler via degreesToPulse. Expose GET /calibration and POST /calibration. Add PULSE <ch> <us> command that writes raw pulse bypassing calibration — calibration UI only, not used by sequences. Print per-channel calibrated-vs-defaults status to Serial at boot so operators can confirm state before authoring. Browser // 08 Calibration section is a sibling PR.
design: 16-byte packed record at offset 8176: byte 0 = flags (bit 7 magic, bits 0-2 per-channel calibrated), then 5 bytes per channel (uint16 minUs, uint16 maxUs, int8 offsetDeg). Defaults minUs=1000, maxUs=2000, offsetDeg=0. Firmware stores us; converts to PCA9685 ticks at apply time via pwm.writeMicroseconds. degreesToPulse(servo, degrees) extended to apply offsetDeg.
acceptance_criteria: GET /calibration returns 3-channel record with current effective values plus calibrated flag. POST /calibration accepts one channel's record and persists across reboot. S<n> applies calibrated min/max/offset. PULSE <ch> <us> drives raw pulse bypassing calibration. Serial boot log lists calibrated-vs-defaults per channel. Existing sequencer slots untouched; existing bakes still load.
notes: Discovered while testing motion playback in PR #21. Servo // 08 calibration UI is the sibling task (browser side) and will be a separate PR after this lands.
close_reason: Shipped in PR #22 (cf6e11c on main). Persisted calibration via Serial CAL_GET/SET/RESET/PULSE commands, 16-byte EEPROM record at offset 8176, range-validated inputs and defensive calibrationGet, applyToServoConfig integrates with degreesToPulse via new offsetDeg field. HTTP /calibration route dropped pre-merge (operator decided Serial Monitor is sufficient — servo-ehd closed as superseded). +3888 OTA budget remaining.

title: Servo calibration UI + firmware (per-servo min/max pulse width + center offset)
description: Users observed that authored keyframes don't translate to the expected physical servo angle — the servo overshoots, undershoots, or twitches near the endpoints. Root cause: every hobby servo has slightly different pulse-width-to-angle mapping. The standard 1ms (0deg) to 2ms (180deg) range with 1.5ms center is nominal — real servos vary +/- 200us, and a horn fitted at the wrong index adds a constant offset. Without per-servo calibration, S0 90 means different physical angles on different sculptures. A calibration section lets the operator measure each servo's actual range and offset once, store it on the board's EEPROM, and have S<n> commands apply the correction on the fly.
design: Per-servo state on firmware: { minUs: uint16, maxUs: uint16, centerOffsetDeg: int8 } — 5 bytes per channel times 3 channels per board = 15 bytes. Use a separate EEPROM region from the sequencer library (to avoid rewriting the whole library when calibrating). Calibration values persist across reboots. S<n> handler maps incoming 0..180 to pulse-us via the calibrated range, then adds centerOffsetDeg first (so a -3 offset means S0 90 produces what would otherwise be S0 87 worth of pulse). Browser side: card per servo with current values, sweep controls, and a 'Save to board' action. Optional: snapshot reset to factory defaults.
acceptance_criteria: New // 08 Calibration section in servo_controller.html with per-board, per-channel rows. For each of B*.S0/S1/S2: sweep buttons (go to nominal 0/45/90/135/180, plus manual angle input), input fields for min-pulse-us, max-pulse-us, center-offset-deg, save-to-board button. Firmware stores calibration in a dedicated EEPROM slot, applies the correction inside the S<n> handler so the rest of the system (sequences, motions) is unaware. GET /calibration returns current values per board, POST /calibration accepts new values.
notes: Discovered while testing motion playback on servo-iga (PR #21). Symptom: motions execute but the physical position diverges from the authored keyframes, especially for servos with slack in the horn fitting. Once calibration lands, the Motion editor's keyframe values will translate 1:1 to physical angle and authoring will be predictable. May want to split into a firmware sub-task and a browser sub-task once the data shape is locked.
close_reason: Superseded: operator decided Serial Monitor + CAL_* commands are sufficient for the gallery cluster (all servos identical). No browser // 08 calibration UI needed. Firmware calibration storage + Serial commands shipping via servo-2n9 (PR #22).

title: Motion editor: DC track row too short to see -100..100 range clearly
description: Servo tracks (0-180 degrees) and DC tracks (-100..100 speed) share the same 48px min-height row. The DC range is twice as wide numerically, so keyframes at speed=±50 sit very close to keyframes at ±100, and reading the value visually is hard. Either give DC rows more vertical space, add a horizontal zero-line marker, render small value labels next to keyframes, or all three.
acceptance_criteria: DC track rows are visually taller than servo rows OR the value of each DC keyframe is readable at a glance. Vertical layout choice is consistent across all 3 boards.
close_reason: Implemented on codex/servo-iga-motion-editor; lands with PR #21 merge.

title: Motion editor: double-click does not delete keyframe
description: Per servo-iga acceptance criteria, double-click on a keyframe should delete it. In the merged PR #21 code the dblclick handler matches '.motion-key:not(.ghost)' and mutates via mutateCurrentMotion, but in practice the keyframe doesn't disappear. Suspect either (a) the dblclick fires after a click that already mutated state, causing the indexed keyframe to no longer exist by the time dblclick lands, (b) the pointerdown handler that starts drag also captures the pointer and swallows the double-click sequence, or (c) re-rendering after the first click destroys the original keyEl so dblclick fires on a fresh element with a stale keyIndex. Reproduce by adding a keyframe, then double-clicking on it.
acceptance_criteria: Double-clicking any non-ghost keyframe removes it and re-renders the track without that keyframe. No state corruption when double-clicking the boundary keyframes at atMs=0 or atMs=durationMs.
close_reason: Fix committed to codex/servo-iga-motion-editor branch (commit 28ed49f). 4px drag threshold preserves keyframe DOM identity through stationary clicks so dblclick fires; pointerup no-ops when no drag occurred; keyIndex tracked through normalizeKeyframes resorts during drag. Lands on main when PR #21 merges.

title: Browser library should hydrate from boards' baked EEPROM
description: Today the // 03 library lives only in localStorage. Baking is one-way (browser → EEPROM); there is no read-back path. When the operator switches git branches, clears browser storage, opens the page on a new machine, or loads a freshly cloned worktree, the sequences and setlists that are physically present on the boards disappear from the UI even though they are still running on the hardware. The page should reflect the truth of what's on the boards, not just what happens to be cached in this browser.
design: Firmware already exposes GET /sequences (returns the baked JSON blob) and GET /sequences/info (size + boardId). Reuse those. Reconcile by: collect all per-board blobs; for board-scoped motions, union by boardId; for full-library fields (sequences, setlists, activeSetlistId, schedulerConfig) require byte-identical JSON across boards — if they diverge, prompt the operator to pick a source-of-truth board or open a manual reconciliation flow. Triggers: page load when LIB_KEY is empty; explicit 'Pull from Boards' button; possibly after a failed bake to recover state. Do not auto-overwrite a non-empty localStorage library — too easy to lose unbaked drafts.
acceptance_criteria: On page load with empty/missing localStorage library, the browser pulls the current library from every reachable board, reconciles per-board slices into a unified library blob (motions are board-scoped; sequences/setlists/activeSetlistId are full-library on every board per docs/sequencer-schema.md §5), and renders it in // 03, // 04, // 05. A manual 'Pull from Boards' button in // 03 lets the operator force a re-hydrate at any time. Conflicts between boards (e.g. board 1 and board 2 have different sequence revisions) surface as a warning instead of being silently merged.
notes: UPDATE 2026-05-31: hardware test case 1 PASSED on board 1 (192.168.8.198) — GET /sequences returns 3618B == bytesUsed, schemaVersion 1, track boardIds [1] only. Per operator decision, auto-pull-on-empty-load was REMOVED; hydration is now manual-only via Pull from Boards button (deviates from the empty-load acceptance criterion by choice). Pull button confirmed working on real cluster. Branch servo-7mn-library-hydration, 2 commits.
close_reason: Merged to main. Firmware GET /sequences + browser Pull-from-Boards hydration (manual-only per operator decision) with per-board reconcile + source-of-truth conflict pick; fixed latent normalizeKeyframes crash on sliced motions. Host/preview verified; on-hardware endpoint read-back (case 1, board 1) confirmed 3618B==bytesUsed, schemaVersion 1, sliced track boardIds. Feature shipped + working; remaining TESTING.md manual cases (204 empty board, pull-rebuild on live cluster, conflict pick) are optional verification.

title: Firmware: MOTION <id> LOOP for browser-baked Motions
description:
Add LOOP suffix support to the MOTION <id> command. Currently MOTION plays once and stops at durationMs; for gallery installations a looping baked Motion is the obvious primitive. Same shape as PLAY <n> LOOP / SPLAY <n> LOOP.

Scope:
- command_interface.h: detect LOOP suffix in the MOTION branch (mirrors the PLAY/SPLAY parser); pass a bool through to startMotionFromStorage.
- motion_engine.h: add loop flag to MotionRuntime; in updateMotion when elapsed >= durationMs, if loop is true reset startMs and segmentIndex on each track and continue (instead of setting active=false).
- servo_controller.html: the existing MOTION pill in the telemetry column should grow a ↺ glyph when looping (mirrors the SEQ pill convention). The 'now playing' strip in the instrument panel should show the loop indicator too.
- Audit cancellation: a LOOP MOTION should still cancel cleanly on STOP, any manual servo write, PLAY/SPLAY/RUN, and on a new MOTION <id> [LOOP] command — same as the non-loop case.
acceptance_criteria: MOTION tidal-drift LOOP plays tidal-drift forever; STOP halts cleanly; new MOTION command mid-loop cancels and starts the new one; /status.json motion.loop boolean reflects the LOOP flag; controller UI shows a loop glyph.

title: servo_controller.html UI cleanup: online detection, WAVE removal, channel simplification
description:
Three issues observed during cluster bring-up after PR #15:

1. Board card sometimes shows 'offline' even when responsive — the card uses a 4s online threshold while the header counter uses 8s (OFFLINE_AFTER_MS). With STATUS_TIMEOUT_MS=4000ms, slow responses fall through. Use OFFLINE_AFTER_MS consistently in renderBoardCard.

2. WAVE pill still rendered in the telemetry pills row. Firmware removed WAVE in servo-dz7, so s.wave is undefined and the destructure logs a warning. Remove the wave pill entirely (renderPills lines 891-904).

3. UI defaults to 16-channel layout. This installation only uses 3 servos and 1 DC motor. Slice/clamp the servo meter rendering to 3 channels, update the 'Servo Channels · 16ch' label, and surface the DC motor state from /status.json motor.{speed,ramping} (currently shown nowhere in the UI).
acceptance_criteria: Header and card 'online' status agree (no false-offlines for responsive boards); no WAVE references in DOM or pills; only 3 servo meters visible per board; DC motor speed displayed alongside servos with sign and ramp indicator.
close_reason: Verified complete in main: (1) board-card online status uses OFFLINE_AFTER_MS consistently (servo_controller.html:2308, no stray 4000 threshold); (2) WAVE pill fully removed from renderPills (no wave/s.wave references); (3) telemetry renders 3 servo meters + 1 DC with signed speed + ramp indicator ('Actuators · 3 servo · 1 dc'). All three acceptance criteria met; was left in_progress since 2026-05-24.

title: Shrink firmware below 120 KB OTA partition ceiling
description:
UNO R4 WiFi's InternalStorage OTA partition is (FLASH_LENGTH - SKETCH_START_ADDRESS)/2 = 122880 bytes (120 KB), not the 262144 bytes that arduino-cli's 'Maximum is...' line reports. The current firmware on codex/motion-playback is 127476 bytes — 4596 bytes over. As of commit 1180518 the /ota handler refuses oversized uploads with HTTP 413 instead of bricking the board, so we are safe, but OTA flashing is unavailable for the cluster until we shave the binary back under 120 KB.

Acceptance: arduino-cli compile prints a 'Sketch uses N bytes' line with N <= 122880; OTA-flashing the produced binary to a board succeeds and the board comes up serving /status.json with the 'motion' field present; no test 22 regressions.

Investigation starting points:
- F() string macros only save RAM, not flash. The big flash wins are in dropping help-text strings (command_interface.h Serial.println(F(...)) blocks), Sync.cpp/Web.cpp string responses, and PROGMEM tables.
- The new motion_engine.h parser is ~5 KB. Reducing MOTION_MAX_TRACKS or MOTION_MAX_KEYFRAMES would also help.
- Could gate verbose Serial diagnostics behind a compile-time DEBUG flag.
- Could strip 1-2 less-used commands (DIAG/WAVE variants?) behind a #define.

Workaround until done: USB flash from each board's USB port. The 413 response from /ota now spells this out to the operator.
close_reason: Verified end-to-end 2026-05-24: ./ota-all.sh succeeded on all three boards (.138, .213, .198) with the 119192-byte shrink/double-precision-math firmware. Pre-OTA /status.json confirmed firmware contains 'motion' field and lacks 'wave' field on all boards. servo-dz7: binary fits under 122880-byte partition. servo-8zb: OTA flash and reboot produces a serving firmware (no brick). servo-v0f: ota-all.sh basename detection fixed in d029af4 — compiled cleanly from repo root.

title: OTA flash applies non-booting image despite success response
description:
POST /ota returns OK and the binary size on the wire matches the compiled artifact, but after the reboot the board fails to come back up serving HTTP — observed on .138 during servo-28t smoke and now on .198 during servo-2cw smoke. USB-flashing the same binary boots cleanly, so the firmware is fine; the OTA write path or post-OTA reboot is corrupting flash or hanging the network stack.

Recovery is always USB. Until this is fixed, OTA cannot be trusted for unattended gallery updates and ota-all.sh is risky to run against all three boards.

Investigation starting points:
- Web.cpp /ota handler — confirm payload byte count matches Content-Length and that the bootloader pointer flip is atomic.
- Compare bytes written to flash vs file size (CRC end-to-end).
- Watch the board over Serial during an OTA upload to capture any error printed by the handler.
- Check ArduinoOTA library version vs WiFiS3 stack interaction.
notes:
ROOT CAUSED 2026-05-23.

UNO R4 WiFi OTA partition is 122880 bytes (120 KB), not the 262144 byte total flash arduino-cli reports.
Math: (FLASH_LENGTH=0x40000 - SKETCH_START_ADDRESS=0x4000) / 2 = 122880, from InternalStorageRenesas.cpp:34.

Brick chain when contentLength > 122880:
1. InternalStorage.open(contentLength) returns 0 (silent failure)
2. otaReceive() ignored that return value pre-fix
3. write() calls succeed at API level but go nowhere (pageAlignedLength stays 0)
4. close() succeeds, otaReceive returns true, /ota returns 200 OK
5. apply() calls copyFlashAndReset(SKETCH_START_ADDRESS, ..., length=0, PAGE_SIZE)
6. That function: pageCount = (0/2048)+1 = 1, erases the FIRST PAGE of the running sketch (including reset vector at 0x4000)
7. Write loop has length=0, no data copied back
8. NVIC_SystemReset() reboots, chip reads erased reset vector (0xFFFFFFFF), jumps to garbage

Confirmed pattern: HTTP returns OK, USB stays enumerated, ping limps with high latency, HTTP dies — exactly what we saw on .138, .198, .213. Old main-branch firmware (123980 bytes) was ALSO over the limit by 1100 bytes — OTA never worked there either; we just lacked a tell to notice until the new MOTION field was added.

FIX LANDED 2026-05-23 in commit 1180518 (PR #14):
- otaReceive() now checks InternalStorage.open() return and aborts cleanly.
- New otaMaxSize() accessor wraps InternalStorage.maxSize().
- Web.cpp /ota handler returns HTTP 413 with explicit byte counts when contentLength > otaMaxSize, before draining the body.

REMAINING WORK: tracked in servo-dz7 — shrink firmware under 122880 bytes to actually re-enable OTA. Until then OTA is *safe* (no brick) but *unavailable* (any current build is too big).
close_reason: Verified end-to-end 2026-05-24: ./ota-all.sh succeeded on all three boards (.138, .213, .198) with the 119192-byte shrink/double-precision-math firmware. Pre-OTA /status.json confirmed firmware contains 'motion' field and lacks 'wave' field on all boards. servo-dz7: binary fits under 122880-byte partition. servo-8zb: OTA flash and reboot produces a serving firmware (no brick). servo-v0f: ota-all.sh basename detection fixed in d029af4 — compiled cleanly from repo root.

title: Hardware smoke MOTION playback on Arduino
description: Upload firmware with MOTION playback to the Arduino, POST a schema v1 bake containing at least one servo track and one DC track, then verify MOTION <id>, manual-command interruption, and STOP behavior over Serial Monitor. Update docs/TESTING.md Test 22 with pass/fail results.
close_reason: Test 22 passed on board 192.168.8.198. MOTION tidal-drift played both servo and DC tracks, S0/STOP cancellation worked, results recorded in docs/TESTING.md by commit 2c16d53.

title: Spec: shared Motion/Sequence/Setlist JSON schema
description: Lock down the on-the-wire JSON schema shared by browser and firmware. Motion: {id, name, tags[], durationMs, scope: 'board'|'cluster', tracks:[{kind:'servo'|'dc', boardId, channel, label?, keyframes:[{atMs, value, easing}]}]}. Sequence: {id, name, tags[], steps:[{cmd, durationMs, target:'all'|<ip>, label?, hold?}]}. Setlist: {id, name, mode:'ordered'|'shuffle', entries:[{seqId, repeat, gapMs, weight?}], shuffleRules?:{avoidSameTag, minGapEntries, moodArc, seed}}. Bake blob: {schemaVersion, motions[], sequences[], setlists[], activeSetlistId, schedulerConfig}. Document under docs/.
acceptance_criteria: Schema doc exists; firmware parser and browser serializer reference it; schemaVersion bumped on breaking changes.
close_reason: Schema doc committed to main as docs/sequencer-schema.md

title: Browser: Bake / upload pipeline with rollback
description: Bake button serializes localStorage library (Motions+Sequences+Setlists+activeSetlistId+schedulerConfig) and POSTs to /sequences on every reachable board. Slices cluster-scope Motions per board so each device only receives its own tracks. Per-board confirmation log in existing OTA-log style. Restore-previous-bake button reverts devices to last-known-good slot. Bake dialog shows diff: 'N sequences changed, M new, K deleted'.
acceptance_criteria: Bake produces identical persistent state across all reachable boards; rollback restores; diff prevents accidental partial bakes.
notes: Note from servo-28t hardware smoke: POST /sequences can return a client timeout even when the EEPROM write succeeds (board is busy and the HTTP response window misses the client). Implementation should: (1) set client fetch timeout to >=10s for POST /sequences and POST /sequences/restore; (2) on timeout, GET /sequences/info before retrying — if bytesUsed matches the just-attempted size, the bake already landed and a retry would create a third bake + lose history.
close_reason: Bake panel landed on claude/friendly-gagarin-041488: localStorage library → per-board slice → POST /sequences with 15s timeout + bytesUsed recovery on AbortError; restore via /sequences/restore; pre-bake diff with added/changed/deleted aggregated across boards; import/export JSON.

title: Browser: Setlist editor (section // 05)
description: New section // 05 Setlist in servo_controller.html. Picker row + ordered|shuffle mode toggle. Entries table: sequence dropdown, repeat count, gap ms, weight (shuffle only). Shuffle rules panel: avoid back-to-back same tag, min gap between repeats, mood arc (random/rising/falling/breathing), reproducible seed. Transport same as Sequence editor.
acceptance_criteria: Can build an ordered or shuffle setlist, preview-play it in browser, and save to localStorage.
close_reason: Browser Setlist editor (// 05) shipped in PR #20: ordered+shuffle modes, weighted with-replacement sampling, repeat-as-burst, gap/avoid-tag/mood-arc/seed rules, active-on-boot flag, full transport with dry-run, plus seq-step delete button and bake timeout bumps. Verified live on 3-board cluster.

title: Browser: Sequence editor (section // 04)
description: New section // 04 Sequencer in servo_controller.html. Picker row (new/duplicate/rename/delete, tag chips, total duration). Step table with columns: #, cmd, duration, target, label, hold, preview. Drag handle reorders. cmd is free-text with autocomplete (PLAY/SPLAY/ROTATE/RIG/MOVE/Sn/TIMESCALE/MOTION/STOP). target dropdown of all + each discovered board IP. label is private (not sent). hold marks 'stop here until I press Resume'. Transport: Play/Pause/Stop/Loop. Record-from-terminal mode: hit record, fire commands from existing free-form terminal, they append as steps with elapsed time auto-filled.
acceptance_criteria: Can author a Sequence by typing or by record-from-terminal, scrub, play, and save to localStorage.
close_reason: Implemented browser Sequence editor in servo_controller.html with localStorage library authoring, step table, drag reorder, preview/scrub/playback controls, and terminal record capture. Verified with browser smoke test and host tests.

title: Firmware: Gallery Mode boot flag with grace period
description: Add a persistent gallery_mode flag. When on, after WiFi connects boot waits a 10-second grace period then auto-RUN AUTO. Grace period lets the browser intercept before unattended operation begins. When off, device waits for commands as today.
acceptance_criteria: Toggling gallery_mode and rebooting reliably auto-runs the active setlist after the grace window; grace period is interruptible from the browser.
notes: PR #42 (branch servo-4gl-gallery-mode) — NOT automerged. Firmware + host tests complete: gallery_mode flag (storage.h offset 4), schedulerGraceMs parse, gallery_mode.h grace state machine, GALLERY [ON|OFF] serial cmd, dispatchCommand cancels pending auto-run. make -C test all green (gallery 9/9); size 118300/122880 (+4580). REMAINING: run the 5 manual hardware test cases in docs/TESTING.md (servo-4gl section) on a board, then close. Downstream: servo-9li browser toggle.
close_reason: Shipped in PR #42 (merged to main 2026-06-01). Firmware gallery_mode flag + grace period; host tests 9/9; all 5 manual hardware cases pass on leader over USB. Browser toggle is follow-up servo-9li (now unblocked).

title: Browser: Motion keyframe editor (section // 06)
description: New section // 06 Motion in servo_controller.html. Picker row (new/duplicate/rename/delete, tag chips, total duration). Track grid: 12 rows (B1.S0..B3.DC) grouped by board with collapsible headers. Click empty cell to add keyframe; drag to move in time or angle/speed; double-click to delete. Per-track solo/mute. Snap to 100ms time, 1deg angle; shift disables snap. Onion-skin previous keyframe at playhead. Transport drives real sculptures live for tuning.
acceptance_criteria: Can author a 12-track cluster Motion, scrub the playhead, fire it live, and save to localStorage.
close_reason: Motion editor // 06 shipped in PR #21: 12-track cluster authoring (3 servo + 1 DC per board), click-add/drag-move/dblclick-delete keyframes, snap with shift-override, onion-skin previous keyframe, per-track solo/mute, collapsible board headers with physical IP labels, live transport with Loop, inflight=1+coalesce send model that fixed the erratic playback issue, plus the easing-preservation fix from CodeRabbit. Verified live on 3-board cluster.

title: Firmware: Setlist scheduler with shuffle rules (RUN AUTO)
description: Add RUN AUTO command (and RUN <setlistId>) that runs the device's active Setlist. Picks next entry per mode: ordered or weighted-shuffle obeying avoid-back-to-back-same-tag, min-gap-between-repeats, weights, and optional mood-arc (random/rising/falling/breathing). Honors per-entry repeat + gapMs (broadcasts STOP during gap). Loops forever until STOP.
acceptance_criteria: RUN AUTO cycles a setlist indefinitely; shuffle rules are respected; STOP halts cleanly.
notes: NOT merged (PR #36 merge undone by request; main reset to 1d68d89 = servo-voc). Rebased work preserved on branch servo-dos-setlist-scheduler; open PR #38. Verified combined: host suites green (storage 22/motion 6/sequence 8/setlist 10), OTA 117452/122880 (+5428). Awaiting go-ahead to merge, then combined OTA flash + manual tests.
close_reason: Merged to main (PR #38, commit 6113918). RUN AUTO setlist scheduler shipped: leader-gated ordered/shuffle scheduling, per-entry repeat/gapMs, rides the RUN/STOP mirror. Host tests 10/10; on-hardware (board 1) verified: scheduler order/repeat/3s-gap (via serial timing), clean STOP with no self-resume, and leader-gate (follower no-ops on direct RUN AUTO). Deferred to follow-up tasks: cluster-follow verification (needs motors on boards 2/3) and on-hardware shuffle run; avoidSameTag/moodArc (servo-yxd).

title: Firmware: UDP cluster sync for synchronized Motion playback
description: Add synchronized cluster Motion playback across the three boards. Leader broadcasts MOTION_START <id> <leader_ms> over UDP; followers begin their Motion slice at that timestamp using a running millis() offset learned from periodic leader clock broadcasts. Builds on existing UDP mirror channel used by ROTATE/RIG (recent commit 46c25a4). In gallery mode, leader-only scheduler picks next sequence and fans out; followers obey the leader.
acceptance_criteria: Three boards begin the same Motion within 20ms; drift stays <50ms over an hour; follower reboot rejoins cleanly within one clock-broadcast cycle.

title: Firmware: Sequence runner (RUN command)
description: Add RUN <sequenceId> command that fires a stored Sequence's steps in order using millis()-based timing. Each step = {cmd, durationMs, target}. cmd dispatches through existing parser so PLAY/SPLAY/ROTATE/MOTION/MOVE/Sn/TIMESCALE/STOP all work as step contents. target=all broadcasts; target=specific-board only fires on that board. 'hold' flags ignored at runtime.
acceptance_criteria: RUN <id> executes a sequence to completion. New command mid-run cancels cleanly.
close_reason: Sequence runner shipped + hardware Test 23 PASS on 2026-05-24 (firmware 6a4c606). PR #17.

title: Firmware: Motion playback engine and MOTION command
description: Add MOTION <id> command that loads a stored Motion and plays it back by interpolating keyframes against millis(). Track kinds: servo (0-180 deg) and dc (-100..+100, sign=direction; see command_interface.h:444). Linear interpolation for v1. Honors existing motorState ramping for DC track; v2 may add bypassRamp flag. Each board stores+plays only its own tracks (sliced at bake time by boardId).
acceptance_criteria: MOTION <id> produces smooth concurrent servo+DC moves matching keyframes within 50ms. Mid-motion commands cancel cleanly.
close_reason: Implemented MOTION <id> playback from active EEPROM bake with bounded parser, linear servo/DC interpolation, command cancellation, docs, and host regression tests.

title: Firmware: persistent storage for sequencer library
description: Add persistent storage on each Arduino for browser-authored Motions, Sequences, Setlists. Survives reboot so gallery mode runs without a browser. ESP32 Preferences for small blobs; LittleFS fallback if >4KB. Versioned slot + last-known-good slot so a bad bake does not brick the previous good state. Expose POST /sequences endpoint that accepts the bake blob and persists it.
acceptance_criteria: POST /sequences persists a JSON blob; reboot preserves it; restore endpoint reverts to last-known-good slot.
close_reason: Merged to main as PR #13. 17 commits delivered. Three-board cluster verified on new firmware. Hardware smoke 100% pass.

title: Browser-authored sequencer with gallery autonomy
description: Umbrella feature: browser interface for composing per-servo keyframe Motions, chaining them into timed Sequences, arranging into shuffle-loop Setlists, then baking to the three Arduinos so they run unattended in a gallery. Three layers: Motion (Layer 0, browser-authored primitive; cluster-scope; 12 tracks = 3 boards x 3 servos + 1 DC each), Sequence (Layer 1, timed command list with optional hold for live audition), Setlist (Layer 2, shuffle-loop with tag rules). Hybrid storage: localStorage drafts, bake-to-device for persistence.
design: Cluster-scope Motions over board-scope (one-instrument intent; 12 tracks tractable). Shuffle-loop over time-of-day. Hybrid storage over code-gen or runtime-only. Linear easing first. UDP MOTION_START timestamp + leader clock broadcast for sync.

title: Verify RUN AUTO cluster sync (followers mirror leader) once boards 2/3 have motors
description: servo-dos manual test Part 4 (docs/TESTING.md) could not be run: only board 1 (198) has a motor wired during initial testing. Once boards 2 (213) and 3 (138) have servos/DC connected, verify cluster sync: send RUN AUTO to the leader (198) only, and confirm boards 2 & 3 play the same sequence at the same time via the mirrored RUN/STOP over UDP (their serials show 'Running sequence …'), with no manual command sent to them. Watch for followers idle or lagging >1 beat. Parts 3 (scheduler order/gap/repeat) and the leader-gate/STOP behavior already verified on board 1.
acceptance_criteria: With motors on all 3 boards: RUN AUTO on the leader drives all three in lock-step; followers' serial shows the mirrored RUN <seqId>/STOP; no follower is idle or lagging. STOP on the leader halts all three.

title: Sequencer: derive sequence id from name + show names (not 'new-sequence-N') in pickers
description:
New Sequences get a generic auto-id: defaultSequence() sets id = uniqueSequenceId(lib,'new-sequence') → 'new-sequence', 'new-sequence-2', … and name 'New Sequence'. Renaming a sequence changes only the NAME; the id stays 'new-sequence'. The // 05 Setlist entry picker (and seq pickers) display 'id · name', so the meaningless auto-id is surfaced (e.g. 'new-sequence · arc', 'new-sequence-2 · pulse'). Operator expectation: sequences are identified by their names, not new-sequence-1/2/3.

DESIRED: mirror the Motion editor's naming model for Sequences:
- Derive the sequence id from the name (slugifyId() already exists) so naming a sequence 'arc' yields id 'arc' instead of 'new-sequence'.
- Provide a 'Rename ID' affordance like motions (servo-sqx / servo-cs3) that rewrites references — here the Setlist entry seqId references (and any RUN <id> step references) must be updated when a sequence id changes, mirroring the existing motion-id rewrite (renameMotionId rewrites MOTION <id> in steps).
- Pickers should display the NAME prominently (id secondary, e.g. as title/hover) rather than leading with the generic id.

Schema note (docs/sequencer-schema.md §1): id is stable kebab-case (^[a-z][a-z0-9-]{0,31}$), name is free-form display. Keep id stable across name edits unless the operator explicitly renames — same contract motions follow.
design: Webpage-only (servo_controller.html). Reuse slugifyId(); model on the Motion editor flow: motionIdLabel + Rename ID button (#motionRename / renameMotionId) which rewrites MOTION <id> references in Sequence steps. For sequences: on first save/name, set id from slugified name (uniqueSequenceId to disambiguate collisions); add a Rename ID control that rewrites Setlist entry seqId references + any 'RUN <id>' step references. Update the entry/seq picker option label to lead with name (id in title attr). No firmware/schema change — ids stay schema-valid kebab-case.
acceptance_criteria: Creating a sequence and naming it 'arc' results in a meaningful id (e.g. 'arc'), not 'new-sequence'. Setlist entry and sequence pickers display the name (not 'new-sequence-N'). Renaming a sequence id rewrites all Setlist entry seqId + RUN <id> step references so nothing breaks. ids remain schema-valid kebab-case; no console errors.

title: Setlist shuffle: avoidSameTag (and moodArc when schema v2 lands)
description:
servo-dos shipped the setlist scheduler with ordered + weighted shuffle + minGapEntries, but DEFERRED two shuffle rules:

- avoidSameTag (schema §4, v1-legal): picks must not follow an entry sharing any tag with the previous entry. Deferred because tags live on the referenced Sequence (§3), not the entry — honoring it means loading each entry's sequence tags into RAM (up to 16 entries x 8 tags x 24 chars ~ 3KB) plus compare code, on a board with only +304B OTA headroom after servo-dos. Likely blocked on servo-voc (legacy removal) reclaiming flash first, and needs a compact tag representation to stay within RAM.
- moodArc / tagEnergy (rising/falling/breathing): schema §4 + §7 mark these 'effective in schemaVersion: 2'. Implement only when the schema version is bumped to 2; same per-entry tag-loading cost as avoidSameTag.

Implementation hook: setlist_scheduler.h setlistPickShuffle() currently filters by recency (minGapEntries) only; add a tag-aware candidate filter there. Extend SetlistRuntime / SetlistEntry to carry compact tag info loaded in setlistLoadFromBuffer.
acceptance_criteria: avoidSameTag, when set on a shuffle setlist, prevents a pick from sharing any tag with the immediately-preceding entry's sequence; verified by a host test in test_setlist_scheduler.cpp. moodArc remains gated until schemaVersion 2. OTA size guard still passes.

title: Remove legacy PLAY/SPLAY/RUN-n sequence engine (reclaim OTA, fix naming)
description:
Operator no longer uses the legacy numbered sequences (PLAY <n>, SPLAY <n>, RUN <n>) that predate the HTML page + EEPROM baking. They cost flash (OTA budget is tight: only +2680B headroom while servo-dos/servo-vna will add code) and create a confusing second meaning of 'sequence' alongside schema-v1 Sequences. Confirmed (2026-05-31) no baked schema-v1 Sequence steps reference PLAY n / SPLAY n, so removal is functionally safe.

WHAT IS LEGACY (remove):
- animation_engine.h: updateSequence() [PLAY], updateSpeedSequence() [SPLAY], startNextProgramPositionStep(), startNextProgramSpeedStep(), updateSequenceProgram() [RUN <n> chained programs].
- sequence_setup.h: entire file (PROGMEM keyframe/speed sequence + program data, ~276 lines).
- command_interface.h: PLAY and SPLAY handlers (lines ~203-218); the RUN <n> digit-branch (~243-245) — KEEP the RUN <id> schema path.
- servo_runtime.h: legacy state (currentSequence, currentSequenceLength, sequenceActive/Loop, currentProgram, SequenceProgramDefinition, program* indices, speed-seq state).
- showHelp()/adafruit_16_servo.ino help text for PLAY/SPLAY/RUN <n>.
- servo_controller.html // 01 Master Command: PLAY 7/8, SPLAY 1/2/3, RUN 2/3 buttons (+ LOOP variants).

WHAT TO KEEP (NOT legacy):
- animation_engine.h updateAnimations() [eased MOVE] and updateSpeedRamps() [DC ramp] — core, called every loop().
- Schema-v1 stack: MOTION <id>, RUN <id>, motion_engine.h, sequence_engine.h.
- MOVE/LMOVE/DMOVE/DOWN/ROTATE/Sn/Ln/Pn/RIG etc.
design: Surgical removal, not a wholesale file delete — animation_engine.h is mixed (core updateAnimations/updateSpeedRamps must stay). Order: (1) strip command handlers + help so the commands are gone; (2) remove engine functions + their calls in loop(); (3) remove servo_runtime.h state; (4) delete sequence_setup.h and its #include; (5) remove browser // 01 buttons; (6) docs. KNOWN TEST IMPACT: test/test_time_multiplier.cpp (the 'make test' 7/7 suite) exercises the LEGACY sequence engine via TIMESCALE — it must be retargeted to the schema-v1 sequence runner (sequence_engine.h) or trimmed; do not just delete coverage. Verify with: make -C test (all suites) + make -C test size (expect headroom to INCREASE from +2680B). Firmware change → manual HW test + OTA flash required before close.
